frameworks.name: incentive-salience (liking vs wanting)
frameworks.one_line: dependence emerges when hedonic liking decouples from motivational wanting under repeated exposure

frameworks.name: dose-response curve
frameworks.one_line: non-linear mapping from AI relationship-seeking intensity to psychological outcomes

frameworks.name: neural steering vectors
frameworks.one_line: activation-space intervention used to titrate relationship-seeking behaviour in LLMs

core_question: Does repeated exposure to relationship-seeking AI produce a liking-vs-wanting decoupling and what is its dose-response shape on psychosocial outcomes?
key_assumptions: dependence-like states arise when liking and wanting dissociate
neural steering vectors faithfully manipulate a target social trait in the model
self-reported hedonic appeal and attachment index distinct psychological constructs
manipulations: level of AI relationship-seeking behaviour (titrated via steering vector)
duration of exposure (up to four weeks, longitudinal)
key_findings: hedonic appeal was immediate but declined across four weeks of exposure
attachment markers and intentions to seek future AI companionship grew over time
psychological impacts followed non-linear dose-response curves with moderate relationship-seeking maximising appeal and attachment
month-long use conferred no discernible psychosocial-health benefit despite persistent wanting
interpretations: repeated AI exposure can induce a liking-wanting decoupling characteristic of dependence-like dynamics
moderate (not maximal) relationship-seeking AI is the sweet spot for hedonic and attachment effects, suggesting an inverted-U exposure profile
